description: This figure pairs a scanning electron micrograph (a) with a formation-mechanism schematic (b) to document B2 intermetallic precipitation in a Fe–15Mn–10Al–0.86C–5Ni steel after 70% cold-rolling thickness reduction and annealing at 900 °C for 2 min. Micrograph (a) displays a multiphase microstructure at 10 μm scale with three annotated zones: area (1) shows continuous B2 stringer bands transformed from δ-ferrite that the caption reports was produced from the liquid; area (2) reveals coarser B2 particles (200–1000 nm) transformed from α-precipitates situated at prior austenite (γ) grain boundaries and edges; and area (3) reveals finer B2 particles (50–300 nm) transformed from α-precipitates decorating shear bands inside γ grains. Schematic (b) explains these distributions through two divergent annealing pathways after cold rolling: one where deformed γ grains (10–20 μm) recrystallize into fine grains (1–2 μm) and promote B2 precipitation at boundaries (type 2), and another where no recrystallization occurs so that B2 precipitates preferentially form along retained shear bands (type 3). The figure supports the central argument that the size and site of B2 precipitates are dictated by recrystallization behavior, and that nanoscale B2 particles can strengthen austenitic steels if their precipitation can be controlled, though a robust strategy to achieve such nanoscale dispersions remains unclear.
